title: Causal-Order Identification of Memoryless Sequential Quantum Processes from Restricted Projective Data
abstract: Identifying causal order from restricted projective data is generally nontrivial. When two quantum players interact only through an unobserved environment, the available local measurement statistics are typically not tomographically complete, so the underlying process cannot in general be reconstructed exactly from the observed distribution. As a result, causal direction can be statistically identifiable in some cases but fundamentally indistinguishable in others.   In this work, we determine necessary and sufficient conditions for deciding when an observed distribution is compatible with a memoryless sequential quantum process in a fixed direction. We show that directional conditional-independence structure and the positivity criterion based on the pseudo-density matrix, as developed in recent work by Liu, Qiu, Dahlsten, and Vedral, are not sufficient by themselves. The missing ingredient is an additional algebraic consistency requirement, and together these conditions yield a complete criterion for membership in the memoryless sequential class.   We then specialize to the two-qubit Pauli setting, where the problem remains non-tomographic but becomes explicitly tractable. In this regime, we characterize when the two sequential directions are statistically indistinguishable, and we show by example that positivity alone does not exclude more general memoryful strategies, whereas the additional algebraic consistency requirement does.
summary: The paper provides a complete set of criteria to identify whether a quantum process is memoryless and sequential when only partial measurement data is available. It introduces a new algebraic consistency requirement that is essential to distinguish true sequential dynamics from processes with hidden memory. The findings are demonstrated through a tractable two-qubit Pauli model.
main_problem: Determining the causal order and memory properties of quantum processes when available measurement statistics are not tomographically complete.
main_result: The author establishes that a distribution is compatible with a memoryless sequential process if and only if it satisfies directional Markovianity, positivity of the reconstructed Choi matrix, and a new algebraic consistency condition (C1).
method: The paper uses the process-matrix framework, pseudo-density matrix (PDM) formalism, and distribution-level analysis to identify set-membership for different strategy classes.
model_or_system: A sequential quantum process involving two players (Alice and Bob) interacting via an unobserved environment, specifically analyzed within a two-qubit Pauli setting.
key_observables: Local measurement statistics, joint input-output probability distributions, and temporal correlations.
important_parameters: Two-qubit Pauli regime, restricted projective measurements, and the dimension of the quotient space of the process matrix.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes restricted projective measurements and a non-tomographic regime where the underlying process cannot be reconstructed exactly.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of causal identification under restricted data, develops a mathematical framework using PDMs and Markovian chains, proposes a new algebraic consistency condition (C1), applies the theory to the two-qubit Pauli setting to prove the necessity of C1, and concludes with a discussion on the impossibility of full process tomography in this regime.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to researchers in open quantum systems and quantum networks, as it provides fundamental limits on how much causal structure can be inferred from incomplete experimental data.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Individual (product) strategy: Charlie prepares two independent input states ρ1 and ρ2 and sends them separately to Alice and Bob. No correlation or sequential mediation is introduced between the two players.

FIG. 2: Parallel strategy: Charlie prepares a bipartite input state ρ12 (possibly entangled) and distributes its two subsystems to Alice and Bob in parallel. The correlation comes only from the shared input state, not from a sequential influence between the parties.

FIG. 3: Sequential memoryless strategy (1 →2): Charlie first sends an input state ρ1 to Alice, then transforms Alice’s output into Bob’s input through a single CPTP map Λ1→2. The defining feature is that the mediation from Alice to Bob is described by one fixed channel.
FIG. 4: Sequential strategy with maximized spacing: ρ12 and Charlie are centered exactly between Alice and the joint processor.
